_schema_notes: Read this before adding entries. Each entry describes a known interaction between two substances (supplements and/or medications). The checker matches user-entered substances against the 'a' and 'b' fields (case-insensitive, and against 'aliases') and surfaces the entry if both are present in the user's stack. DO NOT add an entry without a real, checkable source in 'sources'. DO NOT use 'severity: high' unless the source material itself characterizes it that way. Every new entry must follow this exact shape — do not invent new top-level fields without updating this notes block to explain why.

schema.a: string, primary name of substance A, lowercase
schema.b: string, primary name of substance B, lowercase
schema.aliases_a: array of strings, alternate names/spellings for A
schema.aliases_b: array of strings, alternate names/spellings for B
schema.severity: one of: 'low', 'moderate', 'high' — only use a level the source material itself supports
schema.mechanism: string, plain-language explanation of WHY this interaction happens, not just that it does
schema.summary: string, 1-3 sentences, hedge appropriately ('may', 'is associated with'), no dosage or start/stop instructions
schema.sources: array of objects: { title, url, publisher }. At least one required. Prefer NIH ODS, peer-reviewed journals, or established drug-interaction references.
schema.last_reviewed: ISO date string, set when entry is added or last verified

entries.mechanism: Warfarin works by inhibiting vitamin K-dependent clotting factors. Vitamin K intake directly opposes this mechanism, which can reduce warfarin's anticoagulant effect.
entries.summary: Vitamin K can reduce the ability of warfarin to prevent blood clotting. People on warfarin are typically advised to keep their vitamin K intake consistent rather than avoiding it entirely, since sudden changes (increases or decreases) are what destabilize INR control.

sources.title: Dietary Supplements: What You Need to Know

entries.mechanism: St. John's Wort induces the cytochrome P450 3A4 enzyme system, which speeds up the metabolism (breakdown) of many drugs, including warfarin, lowering their effective blood levels.
entries.summary: St. John's Wort has been associated with decreased anticoagulation effect and unstable INR values when taken with warfarin, due to faster drug metabolism.

sources.title: Clinically relevant safety issues associated with St. John's wort product labels

entries.mechanism: St. John's Wort increases serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine availability through reuptake inhibition, similar to SSRIs. Combined with an SSRI, this can push serotonin levels too high.
entries.summary: Combining St. John's Wort with SSRI antidepressants may dangerously elevate serotonin levels, with a risk of serotonin syndrome, a serious condition. This combination is generally considered one to actively avoid rather than just monitor.

sources.title: The Effects of St. John's Wort and its Interactions with SSRI's

entries.mechanism: St. John's Wort induces CYP3A4 metabolism, which can lower circulating levels of the hormones in oral contraceptives.
entries.summary: St. John's Wort may reduce the effectiveness of oral contraceptives, with case reports describing breakthrough bleeding and unplanned pregnancy associated with concurrent use.

sources.title: Clinically relevant safety issues associated with St. John's wort product labels

entries.mechanism: Some chemotherapy drugs work partly by generating oxidative stress in cancer cells. High-dose antioxidant supplements could theoretically counteract this mechanism, though research on real-world clinical impact is mixed.
entries.summary: Antioxidant supplements such as vitamins C and E might reduce the effectiveness of some types of cancer chemotherapy. Anyone undergoing chemotherapy should discuss any supplement use with their oncology team before starting or continuing.

sources.title: Dietary Supplements: What You Need to Know

entries.mechanism: Iron forms insoluble complexes (chelates) with levothyroxine in the gastrointestinal tract, which reduces how much of the thyroid drug is absorbed into the bloodstream.
entries.summary: Iron supplements may reduce the absorption of levothyroxine, potentially decreasing its effectiveness in managing thyroid conditions. This interaction is noted in levothyroxine prescribing information.

entries.mechanism: Calcium binds to bisphosphonate molecules in the gastrointestinal tract, forming insoluble complexes that significantly reduce bisphosphonate absorption.
entries.summary: Calcium supplements may substantially reduce the absorption of bisphosphonate medications used for osteoporosis. This interaction is recognized in bisphosphonate prescribing guidelines.

entries.mechanism: Magnesium (along with other divalent cations like calcium, zinc, and iron) chelates fluoroquinolone antibiotics in the GI tract, forming poorly absorbed complexes and reducing the antibiotic's bioavailability.
entries.summary: Magnesium supplements may significantly reduce the absorption of fluoroquinolone antibiotics, potentially lowering their effectiveness. Antacids containing magnesium have similar effects and are explicitly noted in fluoroquinolone prescribing information.

entries.mechanism: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) can inhibit platelet aggregation through effects on thromboxane and prostacyclin production. Combined with warfarin's anticoagulant action, this may produce an additive effect on bleeding tendency.
entries.summary: Omega-3 fatty acids may have additive effects with warfarin on bleeding risk. The clinical evidence on whether fish oil supplements meaningfully change INR is mixed, but the combination is noted as a potential interaction in anticoagulant management guidelines. People on warfarin who take fish oil supplements should inform their prescribing provider.

sources.title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids — Health Professional Fact Sheet

entries.mechanism: Vitamin D enhances intestinal calcium absorption. Thiazide diuretics reduce urinary calcium excretion by the kidneys. Both effects together raise circulating calcium levels, and the combination may lead to hypercalcemia — elevated blood calcium.
entries.summary: Combining vitamin D supplements with thiazide diuretics may increase the risk of hypercalcemia (high blood calcium), because both raise calcium levels through different mechanisms. This interaction is recognized in vitamin D prescribing information and is noted in the NIH ODS vitamin D fact sheet.

sources.title: Vitamin D — Health Professional Fact Sheet

entries.mechanism: ACE inhibitors and angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs) both reduce aldosterone secretion, which decreases the kidney's ability to excrete potassium. Taking supplemental potassium on top of this impaired excretion can lead to hyperkalemia — elevated blood potassium levels, which can affect heart rhythm.
entries.summary: Potassium supplements combined with ACE inhibitors or ARBs may cause hyperkalemia (high blood potassium), which can disrupt heart rhythm. Both drug classes increase potassium retention through the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, so additional supplemental potassium warrants monitoring. This interaction is documented in the NIH ODS potassium fact sheet.

entries.mechanism: Garlic (allicin and related organosulfur compounds) inhibits platelet aggregation and may enhance fibrinolytic activity. Combined with warfarin's anticoagulant action, the additive effect on bleeding tendency may increase bleeding risk.
entries.summary: Garlic supplements may have additive effects with warfarin on bleeding risk through platelet inhibition. The NIH ODS garlic fact sheet notes this interaction and recommends that people on anticoagulants inform their provider about garlic supplement use.

entries.mechanism: Zinc, like magnesium and calcium, is a divalent cation (Zn²⁺) that chelates fluoroquinolone antibiotics in the gastrointestinal tract, forming poorly absorbed complexes. This reduces the antibiotic's bioavailability and may lower its effectiveness against infection.
entries.summary: Zinc supplements may significantly reduce the absorption of fluoroquinolone antibiotics through chelation in the GI tract. The NIH ODS zinc fact sheet documents this interaction; the effect is similar to calcium and magnesium interacting with the same antibiotic class.

entries.mechanism: Ginkgo biloba contains ginkgolides that inhibit platelet activating factor (PAF), reducing platelet aggregation. This antiplatelet effect, combined with warfarin's anticoagulant action, may produce an additive increase in bleeding risk.
entries.summary: Ginkgo biloba may have additive effects with warfarin on bleeding risk due to its antiplatelet properties. The NCCIH notes this interaction and advises caution when combining ginkgo with anticoagulants or antiplatelet drugs.

sources.title: Ginkgo — Herbs at a Glance
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Potassium-sparing diuretics work by blocking aldosterone receptors (spironolactone, eplerenone) or by directly blocking epithelial sodium channels in renal tubules (amiloride, triamterene), both of which reduce urinary potassium excretion. Adding supplemental potassium to this already-impaired excretion can cause clinically significant hyperkalemia.
entries.summary: Potassium supplements taken with potassium-sparing diuretics may cause hyperkalemia (dangerously elevated blood potassium), which can disrupt heart rhythm. Potassium-sparing diuretics already substantially reduce potassium excretion; supplemental potassium may push levels further. This interaction is documented in the NIH ODS potassium fact sheet.

entries.mechanism: Zinc is a divalent cation that forms insoluble chelate complexes with tetracycline antibiotics in the gastrointestinal tract, substantially reducing antibiotic absorption. This is the same chelation mechanism by which magnesium, calcium, and iron reduce antibiotic absorption.
entries.summary: Zinc supplements may significantly reduce the absorption of tetracycline antibiotics — including doxycycline and minocycline — by forming chelate complexes in the GI tract. This interaction is documented in the NIH ODS zinc fact sheet and is recognized in tetracycline prescribing information.

entries.mechanism: Calcium ions bind to levothyroxine in the gastrointestinal tract, forming insoluble complexes that reduce how much of the thyroid hormone is absorbed into the bloodstream. This is the same chelation mechanism through which iron reduces levothyroxine absorption.
entries.summary: Calcium supplements may reduce the absorption of levothyroxine, potentially decreasing its effectiveness for thyroid conditions. The NIH ODS calcium fact sheet notes this interaction; the standard advice is to separate levothyroxine from calcium supplements.

entries.mechanism: Melatonin promotes sleep by acting on MT1 and MT2 receptors in the suprachiasmatic nucleus, suppressing the brain's alerting signal and lowering core body temperature. This CNS-depressant effect is additive with other sedating substances — benzodiazepines, non-benzodiazepine sleep aids, sedating antihistamines, opioids, and alcohol — all of which independently suppress CNS function through different mechanisms.
entries.summary: Melatonin combined with CNS depressants such as benzodiazepines, sedative-hypnotics, or alcohol may produce additive sedation, impaired coordination, or cognitive blunting. This is a pharmacodynamic interaction: both substances suppress CNS function independently, and the effects add. The NIH ODS melatonin fact sheet documents this interaction category.

entries.mechanism: Garlic supplements can induce CYP3A4, the major hepatic enzyme responsible for metabolizing many drugs including HIV protease inhibitors. CYP3A4 induction increases the rate of drug metabolism, reducing plasma concentrations of the affected medication. Pharmacokinetic studies have shown garlic supplements reduce saquinavir plasma concentrations by approximately 51% — a clinically significant reduction for an antiretroviral where maintaining plasma levels is essential for viral suppression.
entries.summary: Garlic supplements may substantially reduce plasma concentrations of HIV protease inhibitors (especially saquinavir) by inducing CYP3A4-mediated metabolism. A roughly 50% reduction in saquinavir levels has been documented in pharmacokinetic studies. NCCIH and NIH ODS both flag this interaction. People on antiretroviral regimens including protease inhibitors should avoid garlic supplements.

sources.title: Garlic
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)
